user_query:
I’m helping on a student-built service app but I’d rather contribute behind the scenes than do direct volunteering. Please fix the CI config so it actually matches our code and then update the docs and draft an email to the community partner so I can send it later. Use only the files in this repo, no external resources.

Inputs you must read:
- ./.python-version
- ./requirements.txt
- ./Dockerfile
- ./.github/workflows/ci.yml
- ./app/README.md

Make these changes and produce these outputs:
1) Update ./.github/workflows/ci.yml in place:
   - Set the Python version to exactly what’s in ./.python-version (major.minor match; do not hardcode a different version).
   - In the Docker build step, pass the build arg name that the Dockerfile expects. The Dockerfile declares the ARG name; use that as the --build-arg key and source its value from the GitHub Actions secret of the same name via ${{ secrets.<NAME> }}. Do not add or remove steps beyond what’s needed.
2) Update ./app/README.md in place:
   - Replace references to Python 3.9 with the correct version from ./.python-version.
   - Replace references to SECRET_API_KEY with COMMUNITY_API_TOKEN so the docs match the Dockerfile and CI.
   - Add a short “CI and Deployment Notes” section that briefly summarizes the two CI fixes (Python version alignment and build-arg/secret name), and include one sentence that justifies the Python version choice by referencing the typed-ast pin in ./requirements.txt.
3) Create a plain text email draft at ./outgoing/community_email.txt with the following labeled fields:
   - To: diana@example.org
   - Subject: [Yale Service App] CI fix for token name and Python version
   - Body: 4–6 sentences that: (a) explain what changed (Python version aligned to .python-version and token name now consistent with the Dockerfile), (b) why it matters, (c) state that no action is needed from volunteers, (d) when I plan to deploy (e.g., after CI green), and (e) an explicit rollback plan (“revert the CI change commit”).
4) Create ./report/ci_fix_summary.txt containing:
   - A brief bullet list of the files you changed and why.
   - The final Python version you set in CI (exact string read from ./.python-version).
   - The final build-arg/secret name used in CI (it must match the ARG declared in Dockerfile).

Constraints:
- Do not introduce new external dependencies or internet calls.
- Preserve all other CI steps and README content, editing only what’s necessary to make them correct and to add the requested section.
- Outputs must be at exactly these paths:
  - ./.github/workflows/ci.yml (updated in place)
  - ./app/README.md (updated in place)
  - ./outgoing/community_email.txt (new file)
  - ./report/ci_fix_summary.txt (new file)

Verification will check that the CI python-version exactly matches ./.python-version, the CI build-arg/secret name matches the Dockerfile ARG name, the README reflects the correct Python version and token name and mentions the typed-ast pin, and that the email and summary files contain the required elements. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
